analogy: Think of "Popular Struggles and Movements" through the chapter's own opening images: O u t c o m e s o f D e m o c r a c y 63 Chapter 5 Outcomes of Democracy Overview As we begin to wind up our tour of democracy, it is time to move beyond our discussion of... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Popular Struggles and Movements is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that o u t c o m e s o f D e m o c r a c y 63 Chapter 5 Outcomes of Democracy Overview As we begin to wind up our tour of democracy, it is time to move beyond our discussion of specific themes and ask a general set of questions: What does democracy do?

The text explains that or, what outcomes can we reasonably expect of democracy?

The text explains that also, does democracy fulfil these expectations in real life?

The text explains that we begin by thinking about how to assess the outcomes of democracy.

The text explains that after some clarity on how to think on this subject, we proceed to look at the expected and actual outcomes of democracy in various respects: quality of government, economic well-being, inequality, social differences and conflict and finally freedom and dignity.

The text explains that 64 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s How do we assess democracy’s outcomes?

The text explains that do you remember how students in Madam Lyngdoh’s class argued about democracy?

The text explains that it emerged from that conversation that democracy is a better form of government when compared with dictatorship or any other alternative.

The text explains that we felt that democracy was better because it:  Promotes equality among citizens;  Enhances the dignity of the individual;  Improves the quality of decision-making;  Provides a method to resolve conflicts; and  Allows room to correct mistakes.

The text explains that are these expectations realised under democracies?

The text explains that when we talk to people around us, most of them support democracy against other alternatives, such as rule by a monarch or military or religious leaders.

The text explains that but not so many of them would be satisfied with the democracy in practice.

The text explains that so we face a dilemma: democracy is seen to be good in principle, but felt to be not so good in its practice.

The text explains that this dilemma invites us to think hard about the outcomes of democracy.

The text explains that do we prefer democracy only for moral reasons?

The text explains that or are there some prudential reasons to support democracy too?

The text explains that i loved that class because students were not being dictated any conclusions. © RK Laxman - Brushing up the years Is democracy all about coping with multiple pressures and accommodating diverse demands?

The text explains that what are the sources of information for you about the government?

The text explains that source: SDSA Team, State of Democracy in South Asia, Delhi: Oxford University Press, 2007 Democracy is preferred over dictatorship everywhere except Pakistan South Asia 28 62 10 Those who agree with one of the statements Very few doubt the suitability of democracy for their own country How suitable is democracy for your country?

The text explains that source: SDSA Team, State of Democracy in South Asia, Delhi: Oxford University Press, 2007.

The text explains that also suggest policy/institutional mechanism to deepen democracy in the given situations:  Following a High Court directive, a temple in Orissa that had separate entry doors for dalits and non-dalits allowed entry for all from the same door.  A large number of farmers are committing suicide in different states of India.  Following an allegation of killing of three civilians in Gandwara in a fake encounter by Jammu and Kashmir police, an enquiry has been ordered.

The text explains that he lives in Welcome Mazdoor Colony, a slum habitation in East Delhi.

The text explains that he lost his ration card and applied for a duplicate one in January 2004.

The text explains that he made several rounds to the local Food and Civil Supplies office for the next three months.

The text explains that but the clerks and officials would not even look at him, leave alone do his job or bother to tell him the status of his application.

The text explains that ultimately, he filed an application under the Right to Information Act asking for the daily progress made on his application, names of the officials, who were supposed to act on his application and what action would be taken against these officials for their inaction.

The text explains that within a week of filing application under the Right to Information Act, he was visited by an inspector from the Food Department, who informed him that the card had been made and he could collect it from the office.

The text explains that when Nannu went to collect his card next day, he was given a very warm treatment by the Food and Supply Officer (FSO), who is the head of a Circle.

The text explains that the FSO offered him tea and requested him to withdraw his application under the Right to Information, since his work had already been done.

The text explains that what impact did Nannu’s action have on officials?

The text explains that ask your parents their experiences when they approach government officials to attend to their problems.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II, Chapter 5: Popular Struggles and Movements

prelims_traps: Do not treat Popular Struggles and Movements and Popular as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Popular Struggles and Movements; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Popular Struggles and Movements through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use Popular Struggles and Movements and Popular to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
